coredata.dc:title: One-step microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of green luminescent carbon dots as optical nanoprobes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yi
Xiao, Ning
Gong, Ningqiang
Wang, Hao
Shi, Xin
Gu, Wei
Ye, Ling
coredata.dc:description: A one-step microwave-assisted polyol method was developed to fabricate green luminescent carbon dots (CDs). By using sucrose as the carbon source and diethylene glycol (DEG) as the reaction medium, the CDs were obtained within one minute under microwave irradiation. The as-prepared DEG stabilized CDs (DEG-CDs) had an average diameter about 5nm and could be readily dispersed in water with transparent appearance in day light while emitted unique green luminescence upon a 360nm excitation. Moreover, these green luminescent DEG-CDs could be efficiently uptaken by C6 glioma cells and exhibited a low cytotoxicity, suggesting their potentials in bio-imaging applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH and metal ions on the PL of DEG-CDs

Key Laboratory for Neurodegenerative Diseases of the Ministry of Education

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial supports from Beijing Municipal Education Committee ( KM20110025007 ), Beijing Municipal Foundation for the Talents ( 2011D005018000001 ), Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81271639 ), the Basic-clinical Key Research Grant ( 13JL02 ) from CCMU , and the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory for Neurodegenerative Diseases of the Ministry of Education ( 2012NZDJ01 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM (scale bar is 20nm) image and (B) size distribution of DEG-CDs. Inset is HRTEM image (scale bar is 5nm) of a single DEG-CD particle. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
IR spectra of (A) sucrose and (B) DEG-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, PLE (emission at 517nm), and PL (excited at 360nm) spectra of DEG-CDs (Inset: photographs of the DEG-CDs dispersion taken under day light and UV lamp, respectively), and (B) PL spectra of the DFG-CDs with excitation wavelengths from 320 to 420nm of 20nm increments. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Upconversion PL spectra of DEG-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) PL spectra of DEG-CDs at different pH values. Inset: PL intensity as a function of pH, and (B) normalized PL intensities of DEG-CDs in the presence of various metal ions (n =3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Bright field and fluorescence images of C6 cells without (A, B), and with (C, D) incubation of DEG-CDs for 24h. The scale bar is 50μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: One-step microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of green luminescent carbon dots as optical nanoprobes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Biology and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Biology and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Neurodegenerative Disease of the Ministry of Education, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Neurodegenerative Disease of the Ministry of Education

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-step microwave-assisted polyol method was developed to fabricate green luminescent carbon dots (CDs). By using sucrose as the carbon source and diethylene glycol (DEG) as the reaction medium, the CDs were obtained within one minute under microwave irradiation. The as-prepared DEG stabilized CDs (DEG-CDs) had an average diameter about 5nm and could be readily dispersed in water with transparent appearance in day light while emitted unique green luminescence upon a 360nm excitation. Moreover, these green luminescent DEG-CDs could be efficiently uptaken by C6 glioma cells and exhibited a low cytotoxicity, suggesting their potentials in bio-imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are commonly used as optical probes to visualize specific biological events non-invasively. However, the poor photostability of organic dyes severely restricts their applications for a long-term observation. Despite excellent photostability, the use of cadmium and other heavy metal constituents in QDs raises serious health and environmental concerns and dims their potential uses in bio-imaging. Therefore, developing alternative optical probes with high stability and low toxicity is of experimental and practical importance. Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of ‘zero-dimensional’ nanomaterials in the carbon family, exhibit nonblinking luminescence and superior water dispersible. More importantly, CDs are free of heavy metals and hence are less toxic. These exceptional features make CDs promising candidates for serving as optical nanoprobes in bio-imaging applications [1–7]. Moreover, CDs could be used as building blocks to construct fluorescent nanohybrids with multiple functionalities [8].

Historically, the luminescent CDs were accidentally discovered from purification of crude carbon nanotubes in 2004 [9]. Since then, great efforts have been exerted on the preparation of CDs by various methods, such as arc-discharge [9], laser-ablation [2,5], electrochemical oxidation [10], combustion [11], thermal decomposition or oxidation [12–14], and microwave-assisted pyrolysis [15–21]. Among above-mentioned approaches, the microwave-assisted pyrolysis is regarded as an economical and green method, which provides simultaneous, homogeneous, and fast heating that is beneficial in the formation of uniform-sized CDs. Moreover, microwave irradiation is unique in speeding up the reaction kinetics and easy to scale-up without suffering thermal gradient effects [22]. Nevertheless, CDs prepared by the microwave-assisted method typically emit blue luminescence under UV irradiation. In the case of bio-imaging, CDs with blue luminescence might be less favorable as optical nanoprobes due to the autofluorescence. The green luminescent CDs, one the other hand, appear to be an attractive alternative. In addition, such green luminescent CDs offer unique opportunities to gain complementary insights in the relationship between the physiochemical and optical properties. Although the hydrophobic CDs emitting bright green luminescent in toluene have been fabricated by the microwave assisted method [21], it remains a great challenge to prepare hydrophilic green luminescent CDs by the microwave-assisted pyrolysis.

Recently, it was demonstrated that a higher carbonization temperature could lead to a higher graphitization degree of CDs and consequently induce a red-shift of photoluminescence (PL) from blue to green [23]. The carbonization temperature in the microwave-assisted pyrolysis is somehow limited by the use of water as the reaction medium and this might explain that most of CDs produced from the microwave-assisted method emit blue luminescence. Considering that the position of the maximum PL emission can be tuned by the carbonization temperature, it is reasonable to propose that green luminescent CDs could be obtained through the microwave-assistant method by replacing water with a high boiling point solvent.

Herein, a one-step microwave-assisted polyol method was developed to prepare green luminescent CDs. Using diethylene glycol (DEG) as the high boiling point reaction medium and sucrose as the carbon source, the green luminescent CDs were obtained within one minute under microwave irradiation. This allowed us to systematically investigate the distinctive PL properties of as-prepared DEG stabilized CDs (denoted as DEG-CDs thereafter). Moreover, the potential of these green luminescent DEG-CDs as optical nanoprobes was explored.

DEG and sucrose were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

In a typical microwave-assisted polyol synthesis procedure, 3ml of DEG, 1ml of 30% sucrose solution, and 200μl of concentrated H2SO4 were sequentially added into a 10ml glass tube. The mixture was sonicated to form a homogeneous solution and then heated in a domestic microwave oven (750W, Galanz, China) for predetermined periods. When the reaction mixture was cooled down to room temperature, the resultant suspension was centrifuged (5000r/min, 10min) to remove large or agglomerated particles. The DEG-CDs dispersed in supernatant were retained for further purification by dialyzing against double distilled water using a Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off=100) for 24h.

The size and morphology of DEG-CDs were observed by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan). For TEM imaging, one drop of DEG-CD suspension was deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried under vacuum. The size distribution of DEG-CDs was measured on a Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of DEG-CDs was obtained using Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.54056Å) at a voltage of 40kV and a current of 200mA with 2θ scanning mode. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired over the range of 400–4000cm−1 on an IRPrestige-21 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). For IR analysis, sample was dispersed in KBr (2% wt/wt) and pressed as a 13mm disc with minimum pressure. A total of 100 scans were accumulated with a resolution of 4cm−1 for each spectrum. The photoluminescence spectra were collected on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a Xe lamp as the light source. UV–vis absorption spectrum of DEG-CDs was acquired on a UV-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) using a 1cm path length cuvette.

The quantum yield (Ф) of the DEG-CDs was estimated by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 360nm) and the absorbance values (at 360nm) of the DEG-CDs with that of the quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (Ф
= 54%). Specifically, the absorbance values of DEG-CDs and quinine sulfate were measured at 360nm. PL emission spectra of DEG-CDs and quinine sulfate were also recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360nm to obtain the integrated fluorescence intensity, which is the area under the PL curve in the wavelength range from 380 to 700nm. Then, the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance was plotted and a linear regression was attained for each curve with intercept at zero. Absolute values were calculated according to the following equation:
ϕ
X
=
ϕ
ST
Grad
X
Grad
ST
η
X
2
η
ST
2
where, the subscripts ST and X denote quinine standard and DEG-CDs, respectively, Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the linear regression, and η is the refractive index of the solvent (both are 1.33). In order to minimize the re-absorption effects, the absorbance at 360nm in a 10mm cuvette was kept below 0.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and metal ions on the PL of DEG-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A volume of 50μl of as-prepared DEG-CDs (2mg/mL) was added in 950μl of double distilled water with different pH values. The corresponding PL spectra of DEG-CDs at different pH were then acquired upon excitation at 360nm.

Various metal ions, including Ba2+, Ca2+, Na+, Mn2+, Cu2+, K+, Cd2+, Pb2+, and Fe3+, in aqueous solutions (0.1mmol/L) were first prepared. Then, 50μl of as-prepared DEG-CDs (2mg/mL) was diluted with 950μl of each metal ion solution. The PL spectra of DEG-CDs in the absence and presence of various metal ions were recorded upon a 360nm excitation.

The cytotoxicity of DEG-CDs towards C6 cells was assessed by the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. The C6 cells were seeded into 96-well culture plates at a density of 1×104 cells/well and then incubated for 12h. After the culture medium was removed, DEG-CDs with different concentrations were added into each well. Cells were then allowed to incubate for another 24h. Then, the medium was refreshed with 120μl of medium containing 20μl of MTT (5mg/ml). After incubation for additional 4h, all medium was depleted and 150μl/well of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added, followed by shaking for 10min. The optical density (OD) of each well was immediately measured at 490nm on a Model 680 Microplate Reader (Bio-Rad, USA) to assess the cell viability.

The glioma C6 cells were seeded on the coverslips in a 6-well plate at a density of 2×104 cells per well and cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% of fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator at 37°C for 24h. The culture medium was replaced with FBS-free DMEM containing CDs at a concentration of 1mg/ml. The cells were incubated for another 24h, washed with in phosphate buffer solution (PBS), fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde in PBS, washed again with PBS twice, and observed with a DFC420 C (Leica, Germany) inverted fluorescence microscopy. The bright field and fluorescence images of C6 cells with or without DEG-CDs labeling were obtained under the 20× objective with an exposure time of 3s.

Carbohydrates are commonly used as the carbon source to synthesize CDs. Although a detailed mechanism remains unclear, it is proposed that carbohydrates first experience hydrolysis, dehydration and decomposition in the presence of acid and results in soluble compounds like furfural aldehydes, ketones, followed by polymerization and condensation to produce different soluble polymeric products. Finally, the CDs are formed by aromatization and carbonization [24]. Similarly, in this study, CDs with unique green luminescence were obtained through carbonization of sucrose by a one-step microwave-assisted polyol method. Therefore, several parameters (e.g. the carbon source concentration and the microwave irradiation time) may affect the PL properties of as-prepared DEG-CDs and the experiments began with the intention of optimizing the reaction conditions for the microwave-assisted polyol synthesis. As the carbon source, the concentration of sucrose solution was set to be 30% to avoid the high viscosity. Meanwhile, 200μl of concentrated H2SO4 could effectively catalyze the dehydration and carbonization of sucrose. As another key factor, the microwave irradiation time played an important role in dictating the PL intensity of CDs. It was found that certain microwave heating time (about 20s) was required to produce luminescent DEG-CDs. Recall that the carbon source was 30% sucrose solution in which the water was necessary to form a transparent homogenous solution with an appropriate viscosity for carbonization uniformity. Therefore, the heat during first 20s microwave irradiation was almost consumed by the evaporation of water instead of dehydration and carbonization. As a result, luminescent CDs was formed only when microwave irradiation was longer than 20s and it turned out that CDs with the highest PL intensity was obtained with a 50s microwave irradiation. Longer microwave irradiation time, however, resulted in a reduced PL intensity, which is probably due to the formation of large-sized CDs that have poor or no PL. Moreover, a control experiment was conducted using water as medium. It turned out that microwave-assisted pyrolysis of sucrose aqueous solution under same conditions resulted in the formation of CDs with weak blue luminescence. This thus signifies the critical role of polyol in producing the green luminescent CDs.

The size and morphology of DEG-CDs obtained under the optimal microwave-assisted polyol synthesis conditions were observed by HRTEM. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1 A) clearly confirmed that DEG-CDs were well dispersed quasi-spherical nano-dots with lattice spacing about 0.336nm. The size distribution (Fig. 1B) showed that the aqueous dispersion of DEG-CDs had a narrow size distribution with average diameters about 5nm, which is consistent with that observed on HRTEM image.

The XRD pattern of as-prepared DEG-CDs (Fig. 2 ) revealed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ =24° (d 002 =0.34nm) attributed to turbostratic carbon phase and this was further confirmed by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S1). Nevertheless, the 2θ degree of this peak was greater than that of blue luminescent CDs prepared by the microwave pyrolysis using water as solvent [16,20], implying a less turbostratic carbon structure, in other words, a higher degree of partial graphitization as a result of the higher carbonation temperature provided by the microwave-assisted polyol method.

Furthermore, the composition of carbon core and functional groups on the surface of DEG-CD were inspected by FTIR. Fig. 3 presents the IR spectra of sucrose and DEG-CDs. It was noted that the intensive characteristic IR bands of sucrose, such as C–H rocking band at 852cm−1, C–H bending and stretching mode at 1360 and 2923cm−1 became much weaker in the IR spectrum of DEG-CDs and this was accompanied by the appearance of a new band centered at 1625cm−1, which was due to the CC stretching [21]. Such dramatic changes clearly confirmed the successful transformation of sucrose into DEG-CD with carbon core consisting of CC elementary unit. In addition, IR spectrum of DEG-CDs revealed the existing of various oxygen-containing functional groups, such as the broad band at 3300cm−1 due to stretching of –OH (hydroxyl), the peak at 1705cm−1 attributed to the stretching of CO (carbonyl), and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of C–O–C (ether or epoxy) at 1350 and 1200cm−1. These oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of DEG-CDs probably were derived from the oxidation of hydroxyl groups in sucrose during the carbonization process and they are likely responsible for the efficient PL by trapping excitons under excitation and the radiative recombination of those surface-trapped excitons [1]. Meanwhile, the DEG on the surface of CDs was validated by the presence of –CH2 and –OCH3 bands in the IR spectrum.

The aqueous dispersion of DEG-CDs appeared transparent in day light and remained stable for several weeks. Upon excitation under a 365nm UV lamp, the DEG-CDs emitted a strong green luminescence (inset, Fig. 4 A) and the quantum yield was estimated to be 2.4% using quinine sulfate as the reference (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the measurement of PL intensity against excitation time confirmed that the DEG-CDs were photostable as no significant photobleaching behavior was observed upon continuous excitation (λ ex=360nm) for 3h (Fig. S3).

The UV–vis absorption, PL emission and excitation spectra acquired from the aqueous dispersion of DEG-CDs are presented in Fig 4A. A strong absorption peak at 282nm together with a shoulder around 320nm was observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The peak at 282nm was ascribed to the blue-shifted π–π∗ transition of the conjugated CC units [25] from the carbon core, whereas the shoulder was due to the n–π∗ transition of CO groups on the surface of DEG-CDs. Upon excitation at 360nm, the PL emission spectrum of DEG-CDs verified the green luminescence as the appearance of a peak centered at 517nm with the full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) of 80nm. The green PL might be attributed to the combining effect of the emissive defects [1,26] and the decreased band gap [23] of DEG-CDs as a result of the higher graphitization degree. Nevertheless, the exact mechanism responsible for the green PL of DEG-CDs remains to be further clarified.

To further explore the optical properties of DEG-CDs, PL emission spectra excited at various excitation wavelengths from 320 to 420nm were collected (Fig. 4B). It was noted that with the increasing of excitation wavelength, the intensity of PL increased to the maximum (360nm excitation) and then decreased. However, there was no perceptible shift of PL peak at excitation range from 320 to 380nm, indicating that the PL origin of DEG-CDs might be different from most other CDs that show the excitation wavelength dependent PL. Although the PL mechanism of the CDs currently remains continued debated and requires further elucidation, it is generally agreed that the surface status of CDs significantly affects the PL properties, that is, the degree of surface oxidation primarily responses to trap the excitons under excitation, the radiative recombination of those surface-trapped excitons leads to the PL with corresponding energy [1]. Consequently, CDs with less complex surface status afford PL with unvaried energies at different excitations. Therefore, the observed excitation-independent PL emission over excitation from 320 to 380nm implies a relatively uniform surface status of DEG-CDs [27,28].

Remarkably, the as-obtained DEG-CDs exhibited upconversion PL properties as shown in Fig. 5 . Similarly, the upconversion PL spectra showed a fixed emission peak at 540nm and hardly shift as the excitation wavelength varies, suggesting that the emission occurs from the lowest single state regardless of the mode of excitation. Moreover, the intensity of upconversion PL also depended on the excitation wavelength and the strongest upconversion PL was obtained upon excitation at 740nm. These upconversion PL properties undoubtedly provide DEG-CDs an added value in bio-imaging applications.

Next, the influence of pH on the PL intensity of DEG-CDs was investigated in the pH range of 2–12. As can be seen from Fig. 6 A, there is no significant change in both PL intensity and emission wavelength during pH 4–10. However, the PL intensity of DEG-CDs dramatically decreased at either end of pH, that is, 2 and 12. This pH-dependant behavior provided additional evidence that the PL of DEG-CDs might originate from the emissive traps on the surface of DEG-CDs. Moreover, it is worthy to note that such a wide pH stability range makes DEG-CDs valuable for various biological applications.

Additionally, the effect of metal ions on the PL of DEG-CDs was evaluated by recording the PL intensities in the presence and absence of representative metal ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, and Fe3+). The peak position of the maximum emission was not affected upon addition of metal ion. Therefore, the normalized PL intensities of DEG-CDs in the presence of different metal ions were plotted (Fig. 6B). Notably, there was a tremendous decrease in PL intensity upon addition of Fe3+. Such dramatic quenching of PL by Fe3+ might be due to that Fe3+ ion has higher affinity with oxygen atoms on the surface of DEG-CDs [29]. However, no significant change in PL intensity was observed upon addition of other metal ions, suggesting that DEG-CDs could be developed as a sensor for detection of Fe3+ in living cells with high selectivity.

Prior to assess the potential of DEG-CDs in cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of the DEG-CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay. Fig. S4 shows the viability of C6 glioma cells after incubation with the DEG-CDs at various concentrations for 24h. The MTT results suggested that the cytotoxicity of DEG-CDs was negligible up to 1mg/mL. Inspired by the unique PL properties and low cytotoxicity, the DEG-CDs were then introduced into C6 cells for living cell labeling and imaging. As shown in Fig. 7 , C6 cells incubated with DEG-CDs emitted green luminescence upon UV illumination, whereas no fluorescence was detected in control cells (without incubation with DEG-CDs) under same conditions. This thereby demonstrates that the DEG-CDs with distinctive green luminescence possess great promise in serving as optical nanoprobes for bio-imaging applications.

In sum, a one-step microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of luminescent CDs was developed. DEG stabilized luminescent CDs were obtained within one minute without the need of sophisticated equipment and additional surface passivation. The as-obtained DEG-CDs had a narrow size distribution with average diameters about 5nm and could be easily dispersed in water with transparent appearance in day light while emitted unique green PL under a 360nm UV excitation. The green PL is most likely due to the surface oxygen-containing groups and synchronous the higher partial graphitization degree. MTT assay showed that DEG-CDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity towards C6 cells. Moreover, the fluorescence images of C6 cells incubated with DEG-CDs demonstrated that DEG-CDs could be efficiently uptaken by the C6 cells. Therefore, these green luminescent DEG-CDs could be serving as promising nanoprobes for bio-imaging and beyond.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial supports from Beijing Municipal Education Committee (KM20110025007), Beijing Municipal Foundation for the Talents (2011D005018000001), Natural Science Foundation of China (81271639), the Basic-clinical Key Research Grant (13JL02) from CCMU, and the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory for Neurodegenerative Diseases of the Ministry of Education (2012NZDJ01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.10.086.
